title: TopoGlyph 11.0: The Mechanism Module — Statics & Dynamics of Simple Machines
summary: One module in two parts. Part I (Statics) treats a simple machine as a constraint manifold that re-parameterizes a force field while conserving work — the six classical machines collapse to two families under one operator ⇋. Part II (Dynamics) adds energy stores, inertia, and a time axis: machines now store and exchange energy (⇌ₑ), and an escapement ⊺ divides time exactly as a lever divides force. Applications work the dynamic machines and three generative inventions.
prompt: Extend TopoGlyph to develop a framework of simple mechanical machines — then push it toward dynamic machines (springs, flywheels, escapements), fusing the two into a single Mechanism module.

applications.title: Spring–mass oscillator (the atom of dynamics)
applications.prompt: Using the Dynamics half of the Mechanism module, express the simplest possible oscillator — the irreducible unit every dynamic machine is built from — and give its governing law.
applications.response:
```
Ⓚ⟨μ⟩  ⇌ₑ  Ⓢ⟨k⟩
        ⊾φ : KE peaks when PE = 0 (90° apart)
        ω₀ = √(k/μ)
        E = KE + PE ≡ const        (if ⊘d = 0)
        ⊘d ⟹ ↘e^(−ζt)              amplitude decays
```

**What it is.** A mass on a spring — the irreducible unit of every dynamic machine.
Energy sloshes back and forth between the kinetic store `Ⓚ` and the elastic store
`Ⓢ` once per `↻ᵗ`, perfectly out of phase (`⊾φ`).

**Law.** `ω₀ = √(k/μ)` — frequency rises with stiffness, falls with inertia. Total
energy is conserved only when damping `⊘d` is absent; real springs decay as
`↘e^(−ζt)`.

**Glyphs exercised.** `Ⓚ ⇌ₑ Ⓢ` (the central dynamic operator), `⊾φ`, `ω₀`, `⊘d`.

**Insight.** This is the dynamic counterpart of the bare lever in 11.0: the
smallest expression in which the module's defining operator (`⇌ₑ`) appears. Every
clock, engine, and resonator is a decorated spring–mass oscillator.

applications.title: Pendulum (Ⓢ ⥇ Ⓖ substrate isomorphism)
applications.prompt: Apply the module to a pendulum. Show it as the *same* exchange operator as the spring–mass oscillator with the elastic store swapped for a gravitational one, and say why that isomorphism matters.
applications.response:
```
Ⓖ⟨mgh⟩  ⇌ₑ  Ⓚ⟨½Iω²⟩
        ω₀ = √(g/L)        — frequency set by length, NOT amplitude
```

**What it is.** A bob swinging under gravity. Same exchange operator `⇌ₑ` as the
spring–mass oscillator, but the elastic store `Ⓢ` is replaced by a gravitational
store `Ⓖ`.

**Law.** `ω₀ = √(g/L)` — period depends only on length (and `g`), independent of
amplitude for small swings. That amplitude-independence is *why* a pendulum can
keep time.

**Glyphs exercised.** `Ⓖ ⇌ₑ Ⓚ`, `ω₀`.

**Insight.** The isomorphism `Ⓢ ⥇ Ⓖ` (elastic ≅ gravitational store) means the
oscillator pattern is substrate-independent — the same `⇌ₑ` machine works whether
the restoring force comes from a spring or from gravity. This `⥇` (the
isomorphism-detector from TopoGlyph 3.0) is what let Galileo turn a swinging lamp
into a clock.

applications.title: Flywheel (the kinetic capacitor)
applications.prompt: Model a flywheel with the module and explain, via a `⥇` isomorphism, what it does to a fluctuating input torque.
applications.response:
```
►⟨τ ripple⟩ → Ⓚ⟨I large⟩ → ◄⟨τ smooth⟩
   ⊿Q high ⟹ stores through the gaps between power strokes
```

**What it is.** A heavy spinning disk on a shaft. It absorbs an engine's lumpy
power strokes into its kinetic store `Ⓚ` and pays them back smoothly — a buffer
for rotational energy.

**Law.** `KE = ½Iω²`. A large moment of inertia `I` (= large `μ`) makes `ω`
change slowly under fluctuating torque, smoothing `τ ripple` into `τ smooth`.

**Glyphs exercised.** `Ⓚ`, `μ`/`I`, `⊿Q`.

**Insight.** A flywheel is a **low-pass filter for torque** — flywheel :
torque-ripple :: capacitor : voltage-ripple. This `⥇` carries the storage concept
straight across into electronics, and shows that `Ⓚ` is not just "a moving mass"
but a *reservoir with filtering behavior*.

applications.title: Escapement (the machine that divides time)
applications.prompt: Derive the escapement from the gating operator `⊺` over an oscillator, and explain in the notation how a mechanical clock manages to keep time.
applications.response:
```
Ⓢ⟨mainspring⟩ ──⊺── gear train          mainspring wants to dump energy...
                │
            Ⓚ⇌ₑⓈ  (balance wheel / pendulum)   ...but ⊺ only lets one tooth pass per ↻ᵗ

   each ↻ᵗ:  oscillator unlocks ⊺  AND  ⊺ gives oscillator a push that replaces ⊘d
   ⟹ self-sustaining oscillation;  rate set by ω₀, (mostly) immune to ⟨mainspring⟩
```

**What it is.** The keystone dynamic machine — the heart of every mechanical clock.
A store `Ⓢ` (mainspring or driving weight) is metered out one tooth per cycle by a
gate `⊺`, which is unlocked by an oscillator (`Ⓚ⇌ₑⓈ` balance wheel, or pendulum).

**Law.** Each `↻ᵗ` the oscillator releases the gate **and** receives an impulse
through it that exactly cancels its damping `⊘d`. The result is a self-sustaining
oscillation whose rate is set by the oscillator's `ω₀`, not by the drive. High
`⊿Q` ⟹ the rate barely depends on the push ⟹ the clock keeps time.

**Glyphs exercised.** `⊺` (escapement gate), `Ⓢ`, `Ⓚ⇌ₑⓈ`, `⊘d`, `⊿Q`, `↻ᵗ`.

**Insight.** The escapement is to **time** what the lever is to **force**: it
quantizes a continuous flow of a conserved quantity through a constraint. The
lever's constraint is spatial (`△`); the escapement's is temporal (`⊺`). "A clock
is a machine for dividing time" is the exact `⥇` of "a lever is a machine for
dividing force."

applications.title: Governor (regulation by feedback)
applications.prompt: Express Watt's centrifugal governor and show it reusing the negative-feedback element `⟰` minted back in TopoGlyph 5.0.
applications.response:
```
ω↑ ⟹ Ⓚ⟨flyballs⟩ rise ⟹ ⟰ throttle ⟹ energy in ↓ ⟹ ω held at ⌌set⌍
```

**What it is.** Watt's centrifugal governor. Spinning flyballs rise as angular
speed `ω` increases; their rise mechanically closes a throttle, cutting energy
input and pulling `ω` back toward a set point.

**Law.** A negative-feedback loop: deviation of `ω` from `⌌set⌍` produces a
correction opposing the deviation. Stable regulation when loop gain and lag keep
the system inside its `⍭` (dynamic stability range, from TopoGlyph 5.0).

**Glyphs exercised.** `Ⓚ`, `⟰` (negative feedback, inherited from 5.0), `⌌set⌍`.

**Insight.** The governor shows the Dynamics Module composing directly with the
cognitive-feedback layer (5.0): `⟰` was minted to describe stabilizing cognition,
and here it is realized in pure mechanism. Regulation is substrate-independent —
the same feedback topology governs an engine, a thermostat, and a homeostatic
cell.

applications.title: Trebuchet (M3 cross-module fuse)
applications.prompt: Use the generative move-set: fuse a Part I lever with Part II energy stores (move M3) to derive the trebuchet, and explain what it reveals about the 3rd-class lever from the statics half.
applications.response:
Generated by composing an 11.0 lever with 11.0-D energy stores:

```
Ⓖ⟨counterweight⟩ ──►══△══◄── Ⓚ⟨projectile⟩      3rd-class geometry on the load arm
   Ⓖ → ⇌ₑ → Ⓚ ,  long arm ⟹ ⊿MA<1 ⟹ trades force for VELOCITY
```

**What it is.** A gravitational store `Ⓖ` (heavy counterweight) drops, driving a
lever whose long throwing arm converts the slow heavy fall into a fast light
projectile (`Ⓚ`).

**Derivation.** The static module (11.0) found that a 3rd-class lever "spends force
to buy speed" (`⊿MA < 1`) and left open *what that is for*. The Dynamics Module
answers: a **projectile launcher**. You *want* `⊿MA < 1` here because you are
converting a slow, heavy `Ⓖ` drop into a fast, light `Ⓚ` throw — velocity is the
goal, not force.

**Glyphs exercised.** `Ⓖ → ⇌ₑ → Ⓚ`, lever `►══△══◄`, `⊿MA<1`.

**Insight.** The trebuchet is the 3rd-class lever's *reason to exist*. It is a true
cross-module machine: the geometry comes from 11.0, the energy conversion from
11.0-D, and only together do they explain the device.

applications.title: Horology tree (M4 gap-fill by oscillator swap)
applications.prompt: Apply the gap-fill move M4: hold the escapement gate `⊺` fixed and swap the oscillator under it. Show the family of timekeepers the algebra generates.
applications.response:
The escapement is the operator `⊺` applied over *any* oscillator `Ⓚ⇌ₑⓈ`. Vary the
oscillator and the same machine generates an entire family:

```
⊺ over  Ⓖ⇌ₑⓀ  (pendulum)        → tower / longcase clock      ω₀ = √(g/L)
⊺ over  Ⓚ⇌ₑⓈ  (balance + hairspring) → portable watch         ω₀ = √(k/I)
⊺ over  Ⓚ⇌ₑⓈ  (tuning fork)     → Accutron / high-Q timepiece  ω₀ high, ⊿Q very high
```

**What it is.** Not one machine but a generated *class* — every mechanical
timekeeper is the same `⊺`-gate over a different `ω₀` source.

**Derivation.** Gap-fill move (M4): hold the gate `⊺` fixed and substitute the
oscillator. Each substitution is a known device, recovered (not recalled) from the
algebra — validating that `⊺` is genuinely the timekeeping primitive.

**Glyphs exercised.** `⊺`, `Ⓖ⇌ₑⓀ` / `Ⓚ⇌ₑⓈ`, `ω₀`, `⊿Q`.

**Insight.** Higher `ω₀` and higher `⊿Q` both improve precision — more beats per
second, each less perturbed by the drive. The tuning fork wins on both, which is
why it kept better time than any pendulum: the notation predicts the ranking
before you measure it.

applications.title: Hydraulic ram pump (M5 substrate swap)
applications.prompt: Apply the substrate-swap move M5: take the oscillate-and-gate pattern (`⇌ₑ` + `⊺`) that defines the escapement and put it on a fluid. Identify the machine it predicts.
applications.response:
Energy oscillation `⇌ₑ` applied to a fluid column, gated by a check valve `⊺`:

```
Ⓚ⟨moving water⟩  ⇌ₑ  Ⓖ⟨head⟩      gated by check valve ⊺ (water-hammer pulse)
   each ↻ᵗ: flow builds Ⓚ → ⊺ slams shut → pressure spike lifts a fraction to Ⓖ
   no external power; driven by the source flow itself
```

**What it is.** A pump with no motor. A steady supply flow is allowed to accelerate
(building kinetic store `Ⓚ`); a check valve `⊺` then slams shut, and the
water-hammer pressure spike lifts a small fraction of the water to a height (a
gravitational store `Ⓖ`). It cycles automatically.

**Derivation.** Substrate-swap move (M5): take the "oscillate-and-gate" pattern
(`⇌ₑ` + `⊺`) that defines the escapement and apply it to a fluid instead of a rigid
oscillator. The notation predicts a self-driven pulse pump before you name it.

**Glyphs exercised.** `Ⓚ ⇌ₑ Ⓖ`, `⊺` (now a check valve), `↻ᵗ`.

**Insight.** The escapement and the ram pump are the *same machine* — a gated
oscillator — in two substrates (rigid vs. fluid). It confirms the module's central
claim that `⇌ₑ` and `⊺` are substrate-independent operators, exactly as `⇋` was in
the static module.
